text: Hello. I'm sharing a case of a ischemic right colon. This was a patient with multiple comorbidities who presented with severe abdominal pain and septic shock about a month earlier. He treated with antibiotics, conservative therapy and gets better. Surgery was declined at that time and then the patient actually represented with similar symptoms about a month later.

The scan reveals significant thickening of the cecum extending to the right colon and hepatic flexure. The cecum appears medial and intraperitoneal. The ascending colon is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, and the terminal ileum is visible.
The surgeon begins a lateral to medial dissection of the right colon, which is self-adherent to the anterior abdominal wall and elevated, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon selects a distal margin and uses a vessel sealer to transect the mid-transverse colon, splitting the omentum at this level.

The surgeon dissects along the bowel wall, taking the mesentery close to the tissue. They proceed through the terminal ileum mesentery, approaching the hepatic flexure by dissecting from both sides and meeting in the middle.

The transverse colon is visibly looped, and the surgeon returns to the ascending colon site. The duodenum is carefully swept down from the back of the mesentery, revealing the moving portion of the colon.

The surgeon has completed the removal of the mesentery and is now focusing on detaching the remaining lateral attachments of the colon. The duodenum has been swept down, and the transverse colon is visible.
The surgeon divides the margins of the right colon, preparing for an anastomosis. The right colon is then detached from its adherence to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon identifies a perforation into the anterior abdominal wall, with the bowel appearing liquefied. The colon is seen adhering to the liver, and the surgeon works on separating the last lateral attachments and the omentum from the liver.

The surgeon identifies the area of perforation and prepares for an isoperistaltic ureteral anastomosis. The stapler is introduced through the single incision at the twelve port, which is upsized to the Pfannenstiel incision. The surgeon uses a white load stapler to create the common channel, minimizing bleeding risk in the fully anticoagulated patient.

The surgeon sews the common channel closed, inspecting the interior of the colon. The mucosa appears pink and well-perfused, indicating no signs of ischemia. The suturing begins at the near corner and progresses outward to the far corner.
The surgeon performs an over-and-over suture technique on the anterior row of the colon, using a numbered suture to secure the closure.
